page: Microsoft Azure D8pds_v6 is an ARM-based virtual machine belonging to the Dpdsv6 server family. It is powered by a Microsoft ARM Neoverse-N2 processor featuring 8 dedicated physical cores with 1.0 thread per core, operating at a clock speed of 3.4 GHz. The CPU configuration includes a 64 KB L1d cache, 1.0 MB L2 cache, and 128 MB L3 cache. The server is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding 4.0 GB of RAM per core. For local storage, it bundles a 472 GB local disk and supports Premium Storage. It operates under the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor and does not include any GPU hardware.

Benchmark results place the D8pds_v6 mostly in the average tier across standard compute and memory tasks. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU performance scores are 2073.49 and 16570.31 respectively, both falling within the middle 50% of tested systems. The server demonstrates strong single-threaded gzip compression performance at 33.04 GB/sec, placing it in the top 25%. However, cryptographic operations present a notable performance tradeoff, with the PassMark CPU encryption test scoring 1493.18, which is categorized in the bottom 25%. Memory performance is a key strength, as evidenced by a PassMark memory score of 3089.48, placing it in the top 10% tier, supported by a read-only small block memory bandwidth of 910.15 GB/sec.

With a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio and dedicated ARM cores, the D8pds_v6 offers a resource-dense profile suitable for general-purpose workloads, web serving, and database operations. The PassMark database operations benchmark scored 9902.31, and Redis SET operations reached 15.9 million operations per second, indicating solid capability for key-value stores and relational databases. Static web serving throughput is measured at 13.36 GB/sec. While the system handles light language model tasks, such as prompt processing for a small 135M model at 480.48 tokens per second, it struggles with larger models, showing poor prompt processing performance of 12.33 tokens per second on a medium 7B model. This server represents a cost-efficient option for ARM-native development, static web hosting, and database workloads requiring local SSD storage without the overhead of x86 licensing.
description: Microsoft Azure D8pds_v6 is an ARM-based virtual machine from the Dpdsv6 family, configured with 8 dedicated Microsoft Neoverse-N2 physical cores running at 3.4 GHz, 32.0 GB of system memory, and a 472 GB local disk. Operating under Microsoft Hyper-V, this server lacks GPU acceleration but offers a balanced 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio. Benchmark data indicates average CPU performance, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 16570.31, but highlights top-tier memory performance, scoring in the top 10% on PassMark memory tests. While single-threaded gzip compression is strong at 33.04 GB/sec, cryptographic performance is a tradeoff, scoring in the bottom 25% for encryption. The server is suited for general-purpose workloads, static web serving, and database operations, achieving 15.9 million operations per second in Redis SET benchmarks. It represents a resource-dense, cost-efficient option for ARM-native development and database hosting.
og_description: Microsoft Azure D8pds_v6 is an ARM-based virtual machine featuring 8 dedicated Neoverse-N2 cores, 32 GB RAM, and a 472 GB local disk, offering balanced performance for general-purpose and database workloads.
meta_description: Microsoft Azure D8pds_v6 is an ARM-based virtual machine with 8 dedicated cores, 32 GB RAM, and 472 GB local storage for general-purpose workloads.
tagline: An ARM-powered virtual machine featuring dedicated physical cores, high-bandwidth memory, and local storage for balanced general-purpose and database workloads.
bullet_points: Powered by 8 dedicated Microsoft Neoverse-N2 ARM cores at 3.4 GHz
Equipped with 32.0 GB RAM and a 472 GB local storage disk
Top-tier memory performance ranking in the top 10% of benchmarks
Strong single-threaded gzip compression performance at 33.04 GB/sec
Suited for general-purpose applications, web serving, and database operations
Lower performance in cryptographic encryption and large LLM inference
